eras.name: Pre-Colonial Dynastic Era
eras.description: A period defined by status-based dressing where textiles like hand-woven Aso Oke and Akwete signified royalty and lineage. Garments were voluminous, utilizing heavy hand-loomed cotton and silk to project power and wealth.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Cinematic portrait of a 15th-century Benin Kingdom noble, wearing elaborate coral bead necklaces and high-collared headpieces, draped in heavy hand-woven indigo textiles with intricate geometric patterns, historical accuracy, royal courtyard background.

eras.name: Colonial Synthesis
eras.description: The introduction of Victorian silhouettes and European tailoring merged with indigenous fabrics. Saro and Amaro returnees from Sierra Leone and Brazil influenced a hybrid style of lace, top hats, and traditional wrappers.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1920s Lagos socialite wearing a high-neck Victorian lace blouse paired with a traditional hand-woven Aso Oke wrapper, carrying a parasol, vintage sepia tones, colonial architecture background.

eras.name: Independence Golden Age
eras.description: Post-independence optimism fueled a boom in high-society fashion. The 'Iro and Buba' became a symbol of national pride, often rendered in expensive Swiss lace or George fabric, while the 'Afro' aesthetic emerged.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1960s Nigerian woman in a vibrant yellow lace Iro and Buba with a massive, expertly tied skyscraper Gele headtie, mid-century modern furniture, Kodachrome film style.

eras.name: The Oil Boom & Power Dressing
eras.description: Economic shifts led to an era of 'more is more.' Nigerian fashion saw exaggerated shoulder pads, heavy embroidery on Agbadas, and the rise of organized fashion shows in Lagos hotels.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1980s Nigerian businessman in a voluminous white Agbada with heavy gold silk embroidery, wearing a velvet Fila hat, standing next to a Mercedes-Benz, 80s aesthetic.

eras.name: Modern Nigerian Renaissance
eras.description: The global explosion of Nollywood and Afrobeats exported Nigerian style. Designers began deconstructing traditional silhouettes, leading to the 'Alté' movement and the professionalization of the industry.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Modern Nigerian runway model wearing a deconstructed Aso Oke gown with structural architectural shoulders, high-fashion editorial lighting, Lagos Fashion Week runway.

eras.name: Digital Global Dominance
eras.description: Nigerian fashion is now a global powerhouse, characterized by high-tech textile manipulation and a blend of streetwear with luxury heritage. It is the era of the 'Global African' identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Contemporary Lagos street style, youth in oversized Adire-print puffer jackets and chunky sneakers, vibrant urban background with neon signs, high-resolution photography.

textiles.name: Aso Oke
textiles.description: A prestigious hand-woven cloth created by the Yoruba people, typically made of silk or cotton.
textiles.technique: Narrow-strip weaving on a horizontal loom

textiles.name: Adire
textiles.description: Indigo-dyed cloth using resist-dyeing techniques to create intricate patterns.

textiles.name: Ankara
textiles.description: Wax-resist printed cotton fabric known for its vibrant colors and symbolic motifs.

textiles.name: Akwete
textiles.description: A unique sisal and cotton textile known for its wide width and complex geometric patterns.

climate.fashionImplication: High humidity dictates the use of breathable natural fibers like cotton and silk; voluminous silhouettes like the Agbada facilitate airflow around the body.
